items.evidence_text:
Advisor: Okay, around $200k. Is that just you—since it’s just your household now—or is there anyone else’s income we should include?
Client: Just me.
Advisor: And when you say around $200k, is it closer to $212k gross for the year?
Client: Yes—about $211,900 gross annually.
Advisor: Perfect. And how does that break down—salary versus rental income? I know you mentioned rentals earlier.

Advisor: Okay—about $10k. When you say that, is that like net out of your checking, or gross including taxes and stuff?
Client: Net. Like what actually leaves my accounts.
Advisor: Perfect. And just to be precise—are we talking about roughly $10,200 a month, or is it more like $9,500 to $10,000?
Client: It’s closer to $10,200 a month. I rounded before.
Advisor: Great, I’m writing down $10,200 monthly expenses. Now, do you have any monthly debt payments—mortgage, car loan, credit cards you’re carrying, anything like that?

Advisor: Got it—guardrails for near-term needs, but still investing with some edge. Going back to that monthly spending piece—what’s your typical monthly spend right now, all-in?
Client: About ten thousand a month.
Advisor: Okay—about $10k. When you say that, is that like net out of your checking, or gross including taxes and stuff?
Client: Net. Like what actually leaves my accounts.
Advisor: Perfect. And just to be precise—are we talking about roughly $10,200 a month, or is it more like $9,500 to $10,000?

Advisor: Great. Now let’s talk assets—what you’ve got invested and where. Roughly, what’s your total investable assets—so not counting your home—just accounts you can invest from?
Client: Like… one point two million-ish.
Advisor: Okay, about $1.2 million. Is it closer to $1,242,950 total investable assets?
Client: Yes, that’s basically it—about $1.243 million.
Advisor: Perfect. Let’s break that into the buckets you have. You mentioned a taxable account—what’s that sitting at?

Client: Yes.
Advisor: Great. Now, your home—primary residence—what do you estimate the value is right now?
Client: About $700k.
Advisor: Okay, about $700k. Is it closer to $703,300?
Client: Yes, $703,300.

Client: Net. Like what actually leaves my accounts.
Advisor: Perfect. And just to be precise—are we talking about roughly $10,200 a month, or is it more like $9,500 to $10,000?

Client: Net. Like what actually leaves my accounts.
Advisor: Perfect. And just to be precise—are we talking about roughly $10,200 a month, or is it more like $9,500 to $10,000?

Client: One. Just me.
Advisor: Okay—one adult. And you mentioned two dependents earlier, but adult count is one. Got it.
Advisor: Next—taxes. When you think about your federal bracket, are you around the 32% bracket right now?
Client: I think so, yeah—32%.
Advisor: Great. When you say 32%, you mean the marginal bracket, not like your overall effective rate—just checking.

Advisor: Hi Mar—good to meet you. I’m Alex. Before we dive in, how are you holding up today?
Client: I’m okay. A little tired, honestly, but I’m glad we’re doing this.
Advisor: Totally get it. We’ll keep it practical. Tell me what you want to get out of today—like, if we nail one or two things, what are they?
Client: I want a plan that keeps my monthly spending covered and doesn’t mess up the kids’ stability. And I don’t want to be overly conservative.
Advisor: Got it—guardrails for near-term needs, but still investing with some edge. Going back to that monthly spending piece—what’s your typical monthly spend right now, all-in?

Advisor: Got it—guardrails for near-term needs, but still investing with some edge. Going back to that monthly spending piece—what’s your typical monthly spend right now, all-in?
Client: About ten thousand a month.
Advisor: Okay—about $10k. When you say that, is that like net out of your checking, or gross including taxes and stuff?
Client: Net. Like what actually leaves my accounts.
Advisor: Perfect. And just to be precise—are we talking about roughly $10,200 a month, or is it more like $9,500 to $10,000?

Client: Oh—wait, I always mix that up. I mean the bracket, the marginal one. 32%.
Advisor: Perfect, that’s exactly what I meant. Thanks for clarifying.
Advisor: And risk—earlier you sounded pretty comfortable with market swings. Would you describe your risk tolerance as aggressive?
Client: Yes. Aggressive.
Advisor: Okay. And if I’m putting words in your mouth, stop me—aggressive, but with guardrails so monthly spending and the kids’ stuff isn’t at risk.

Advisor: Okay—about $10k. When you say that, is that like net out of your checking, or gross including taxes and stuff?

Advisor: Going back to what you said earlier about wanting guardrails—before we get into the “what I’d do in your shoes” part, I want to confirm a couple household basics, Mar.
Client: Okay, sure.
Advisor: First one’s simple: marital status—are you currently divorced, widowed, single, remarried…?
Client: Divorced.
Advisor: Got it—divorced. And earlier you mentioned supporting two dependents—just to make sure I’m not mixing that up—how many dependents are you claiming right now?

Advisor: And the rental income—ballpark annualized?
Client: About $147,400 a year, but it comes in kind of ad hoc.
Advisor: Got it—variable timing. Just to make sure I’m not mixing terms: when you say ad hoc, you mean not a set monthly paycheck, but it still totals around $147,400 over a year?
Client: Exactly.
Advisor: Great. Now let’s talk assets—what you’ve got invested and where. Roughly, what’s your total investable assets—so not counting your home—just accounts you can invest from?

Client: I want a plan that keeps my monthly spending covered and doesn’t mess up the kids’ stability. And I don’t want to be overly conservative.

Advisor: Perfect—thanks for correcting me there.
Advisor: Now, just to confirm your date of birth for planning—month, day, year—what do you have on file?
Client: November 9th, 1960.
Advisor: November 9, 1960—thank you.
Advisor: And then income—this is the last numeric one I promise. Earlier we talked about the two streams, salary and rental. When you think “gross annual income” all-in, what’s the rough total?

Advisor: Also held at a brokerage?
Client: Yes.
Advisor: Great. Now, your home—primary residence—what do you estimate the value is right now?
Client: About $700k.
Advisor: Okay, about $700k. Is it closer to $703,300?

Client: Sure.
Advisor: Okay—going back to what you said earlier about the rentals being the bigger chunk, I want to make sure I understand your work situation too.
Advisor: Mar, are you currently working—like actively employed right now—or are you fully retired and just managing the properties?
Client: I’m employed. Still working.
Advisor: Got it—employed. And when you say “still working,” is that as an employee somewhere, or is it more that you run your own business?

Advisor: Got it—employed. And when you say “still working,” is that as an employee somewhere, or is it more that you run your own business?
Client: I run my own business.
Advisor: Okay, business owner—thank you. And the salary piece we talked about, that’s coming from your business payroll, basically?
Client: Yeah, basically. It’s my W-2 from the business.
Advisor: Perfect. Just so I don’t mix terms—when you say W-2, you mean you’re paying yourself wages, not like distributions only.

Client: Salary is the smaller piece. Rentals are the bigger piece and it’s not perfectly even month to month.
Advisor: Right. For the salary portion, what does that annualize to?
Client: About $64,450 a year from salary.
Advisor: And the rental income—ballpark annualized?
Client: About $147,400 a year, but it comes in kind of ad hoc.

Advisor: Got it. Let me jot that—primary.

Client: Wait—why would I be Client 0? I’m Client 1.
Advisor: Yeah, sorry—let me re-ask that cleanly. You want to be labeled Client 1, correct?
